intro.overview: LoneStar DevOps has an enterprise prospect requiring a third-party security assessment. Their rapid growth has outpaced their security maturity, and with Series B funding on the horizon, they need to know exactly what vulnerabilities exist in their infrastructure — and which ones to fix first. This week you'll deploy a local simulation of their environment, conduct comprehensive vulnerability discovery, and transform raw findings into a prioritized risk analysis that Amanda Nguyen can take to investors.

mentorQuote.title: Senior Penetration Tester, NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: Scan everything. Version detect everything. The vulnerability you miss is the one that gets exploited. But finding vulnerabilities is only half the job — a scanner output is worthless without context. Anyone can produce a list of CVEs. What separates a consultant from a technician is knowing which ones actually matter to the client.

focusAreas: Docker container deployment and network architecture
Network scanning and service discovery with Nmap
Risk matrix construction and remediation planning
objectives: Deploy the LoneStar DevOps simulated infrastructure using Docker
Conduct comprehensive port scanning and service enumeration
Identify and document key vulnerabilities across all services
Select and score the top 10 findings using CVSS 3.1
Build a risk matrix and prioritized remediation roadmap

callouts.title: Save For Lab 5.2
callouts.text: This is Week 1 of a 2-week module. Create a folder called Module5_Screenshots and save EVERY screenshot there. You’ll compile these into your Lab 5.2 Engagement Packet next week.

callouts.title: This Lab Is Longer Than Usual
callouts.text: This Foundation Lab covers both vulnerability discovery and risk analysis in a single week. Budget 3–3.5 hours and don’t start it the night before it’s due. Checkpoints 1–3 are tool-based; Checkpoints 4–5 are analysis-based and can be done without your VM running.

totalRow.label: TOTAL
totalRow.note: Scan times vary. Nmap NSE and WPScan can take 10–15 minutes each — let them run in the background while you document earlier findings.

timeGuide.timesheetNote: Log this week as Internal Training: 3–3.5 hours — Vulnerability assessment and risk analysis. This is Week 1 of a 2-week module. Your Engagement Packet (Lab 5.2) is due next week.

content.whatYoureDoing: A local simulation of LoneStar DevOps’s infrastructure as a single Docker container running four internal services: their customer portal (WordPress on port 8080), database server (MySQL on 3306), development API (Node.js on 3000), and management access (SSH on 2222).
content.whyItMatters: Vulnerability assessments require a target environment. Docker allows us to create isolated, reproducible simulations of client infrastructure without touching their production systems. This is how NEXUS tests assessment methodology before deploying to client networks.

steps.text: Verify Docker is running:
Pull the LoneStar infrastructure image from Docker Hub:
Run the container with all four service ports exposed:
Verify the container is running:
Verify all four internal services are up inside the container:
Test connectivity to each service (WordPress on 8080, MySQL on 3306, API on 3000, SSH on 2222).

content.code:
# Verify Docker is working
docker --version

# Pull and start the LoneStar container
docker pull jfnewsom/lonestar-startup:latest
docker run -d --name lonestar \
  -p 8080:80 -p 3306:3306 -p 3000:3000 -p 2222:22 \
  jfnewsom/lonestar-startup:latest

# Verify container and internal services
docker ps
docker exec lonestar supervisorctl status

# Quick connectivity checks
curl -sI http://localhost:8080/ | head -1
curl -s http://localhost:3000/

content.description: docker ps + supervisorctl status showing the lonestar container and its four internal services.
content.body: Take Screenshot 01. Capture a terminal showing both docker ps (the lonestar container running) and docker exec lonestar supervisorctl status (all four internal services — webapp-prod, db-primary, dev-api, mgmt-ssh — showing RUNNING). Your VM hostname must be visible in the terminal prompt.

content.description: Connectivity test to at least one service.
content.body: Take Screenshot 02. Capture a successful connectivity test — curl output for localhost:8080 (WordPress) or the WordPress login page in a browser with your VM hostname visible in an adjacent terminal.

items.label: Docker not running
items.text: Start with sudo systemctl start docker

items.label: Port already in use
items.text: If docker run errors on a port, find the conflict with sudo ss -tulpn | grep -E ':(8080|3306|3000|2222)' and stop whatever’s listening, or pick an alternate host port.

items.label: Container name already in use
items.text: If docker run reports that the name lonestar is already taken, remove the old one with docker rm -f lonestar and re-run.

items.label: A service shows as not RUNNING in supervisorctl
items.text: Inspect logs for that service inside the container, e.g. docker exec lonestar tail -50 /var/log/supervisor/webapp-prod.err.log.

content.text: What is the difference between a vulnerability scanner and a penetration test? Research how automated scanning fits into a security assessment methodology.
Add to your references: PTES — Penetration Testing Execution Standard. http://www.pentest-standard.org/
Four running Docker containers (webapp-prod, db-primary, dev-api, mgmt-ssh) with accessible services on ports 8080, 3306, 3000, and 2222.

Review the Virtualization section, particularly Application Cells/Containers. Note how LoneStar’s containerized architecture both enables this assessment and introduces its own security considerations.

content.whatYoureDoing: A complete inventory of LoneStar’s attack surface — every open port, every running service, version information, and known vulnerabilities — in a single combined scan pass.
content.whyItMatters: Service enumeration is the foundation of vulnerability assessment. Running version detection and default NSE scripts together is how NEXUS conducts efficient assessments — one thorough pass beats two partial ones. Version strings reveal outdated software; NSE scripts surface known CVEs and misconfigurations.

content.title: Junior Security Analyst, NEXUS Security
content.quote: My first vulnerability assessment, I missed an open MySQL port because I only scanned common ports. Check your scans twice. Run a full port scan at least once — you’d be surprised what services companies expose on non-standard ports.

steps.text: Identify the Docker network range:
Run host discovery to identify all container IPs:
Run a combined version detection and default script scan against all containers:
Review output and document all services with exact version strings and any CVEs flagged by NSE scripts

content.code:
# Find Docker network range
ip addr show docker0

# Host discovery
sudo nmap -sn 172.17.0.0/24

# Combined version detection + default NSE scripts (full port range)
sudo nmap -sV -sC -p- 172.17.0.2-5

# Targeted vulnerability scripts if you want to go deeper
sudo nmap --script vuln -p 3306 172.17.0.3

content.description: Host discovery showing all container IPs.
content.body: Take Screenshot 03. Capture the nmap host discovery output showing all container IPs identified. Your VM hostname must be visible in the prompt.

content.description: Combined -sV -sC scan results showing services, versions, and NSE findings.
content.body: Take Screenshot 04. Capture the combined scan output showing open ports, version strings (e.g., Apache 2.4.x, MySQL 5.7.x, OpenSSH 7.6p1), and any CVE identifiers flagged by NSE scripts. Output will be long — scroll to capture the most significant findings. Multiple screenshots labeled 04a, 04b are fine.

items.label: Wrong subnet
items.text: Scan the Docker bridge network, not your host network. Verify your target range with docker network inspect bridge

items.label: Skipping -sC
items.text: The -sC flag runs default NSE scripts alongside version detection — this is what surfaces misconfigurations and CVEs without a separate scan pass

items.label: Long scan times
items.text: A full -p- scan can take 5–10 minutes per host. Let it run and document findings from earlier checkpoints in the meantime

content.text: What is the CVE system and who maintains it? Research how vulnerabilities are assigned CVE IDs and what CVSS scores represent.
Add: Nmap Reference Guide. https://nmap.org/book/man.html and CVE — MITRE. https://cve.mitre.org/
A documented service inventory showing WordPress on Apache, MySQL, Node.js API, and OpenSSH with exact version numbers — plus any CVEs or misconfigurations flagged by NSE scripts.

Review the Security Devices sections (Firewalls, IDS, Network Access Control). Note which of these controls LoneStar’s infrastructure is missing, and consider how each gap contributes to the findings your scan surfaces.

content.whatYoureDoing: Deep vulnerability analysis using application-specific tools: WPScan for the WordPress customer portal and MySQL client for database credential testing.
content.whyItMatters: Generic scanners miss application-specific vulnerabilities. WPScan knows WordPress plugin and theme vulnerabilities that Nmap doesn’t. Default database credentials are one of the most common and damaging findings in real assessments — a compromised database is a compromised business.

content.title: Lead DevOps Engineer, LoneStar DevOps
content.quote: I’ve been saying we need to address this for months. Finally someone’s listening. Just be thorough. I need ammunition to get budget for this.

steps.text: Run WPScan against the WordPress installation:
Test MySQL authentication with default and weak credentials:
Check for former employee account on SSH:
Document all findings with severity ratings and note which asset each affects

content.code:
# WordPress vulnerability scan
wpscan --url http://localhost:8080 --enumerate vp,vt,u

# MySQL credential test (try: root/root, root/password, root/[blank])
mysql -h localhost -P 3306 -u root -p

# Former employee account check
ssh -p 2222 jdavis@localhost

content.title: What About npm audit?
content.text: The Node.js container supports dependency auditing via docker exec -it dev-api npm audit. This is a real tool used in production environments — worth knowing it exists. It’s not required for this lab, but if you run it, any high/critical findings are fair game for your Checkpoint 4 CVSS selection.

content.body: Take Screenshot 05. Capture WPScan output showing identified vulnerabilities, plugin findings, or version information. Your VM hostname must be visible in the prompt.

content.description: MySQL credential test result OR SSH former employee finding.
content.body: Take Screenshot 06. Capture either your MySQL connection attempt (success or failure with the credentials you tried) or your SSH test for the jdavis account. Document what you found.

items.label: WPScan API token
items.text: Some WPScan features require a free API token. If enumeration returns limited results, register at wpscan.com for a free token

items.label: MySQL connection refused
items.text: Verify the db-primary container is running with docker ps before attempting the connection

items.label: Too many findings
items.text: WPScan can be verbose. Focus on vulnerabilities with CVE numbers or severity ratings — these are your Checkpoint 4 candidates

content.text: What makes default credentials such a persistent problem in the industry? Research “credential stuffing” and how default password databases are used in real attacks.
Add: WPScan WordPress Vulnerability Database. https://wpscan.com/wordpresses

WPScan findings from the WordPress portal, results from MySQL credential testing, and documentation of the jdavis former employee account. Combined with Checkpoint 2, you should have enough findings to select a strong top 10 for CVSS scoring.

The Chapter 20 sections on Business Risks and Business Impact Analysis explain why the jdavis account and MySQL default credentials rank above many higher-CVSS findings. Start thinking about asset mapping — which container holds customer data, and what’s the impact if it’s compromised?

content.whatYoureDoing: Selecting your 10 most significant findings from Checkpoints 2 and 3, then scoring each using the CVSS 3.1 calculator.
content.whyItMatters: CVSS provides a standardized language for communicating vulnerability severity across teams, vendors, and clients. But before you can score, you have to select — and selection requires judgment. Which 10 findings represent the real risk surface for LoneStar? That decision is more important than the scores themselves.

content.title: Founder & Principal Consultant, NEXUS Security
content.quote: Anyone can run a scanner and produce a list of CVEs. What separates a consultant from a technician is context. Select findings that tell a story about LoneStar’s actual risk — not just the highest numbers.

steps.text: Review all findings from Checkpoints 2 and 3. Select the 10 that best represent LoneStar’s risk profile. Document your selection rationale — why these 10?
Access the CVSS 3.1 calculator: https://www.first.org/cvss/calculator/3.1
For each finding: look up the CVE if available, or calculate based on characteristics. Document Attack Vector, Attack Complexity, Privileges Required, User Interaction, Scope, and C/I/A Impact
Build a summary table: Finding Name, CVE (if applicable), Base Score, Severity Rating, one-sentence rationale for inclusion

content.title: Key Insight: CVSS Score ⁄ Business Priority
content.text: WordPress SQL injection may score 9.8 but only affects the marketing site. MySQL default credentials may score 7.3 but expose ALL customer data. Your selection and rationale should reflect this distinction — it’s what your Engagement Packet will need to defend.

content.description: CVSS 3.1 calculator with at least one vulnerability scored.
content.body: Take Screenshot 07. Capture the CVSS calculator with one of your top 10 findings fully scored. The metric selections and resulting score must be visible.

content.description: Complete top-10 vulnerability table with CVSS scores.
content.body: Take Screenshot 08. Capture your completed summary table showing all 10 selected findings with CVSS scores, severity ratings, and your selection rationale. A Word or spreadsheet table is fine — legibility matters.

items.label: Using CVSS 2.0 scores
items.text: Some older CVE entries show CVSS 2.0 scores. Always use the v3.1 calculator at first.org for consistency

items.label: Confusing Attack Vector with Attack Complexity
items.text: Attack Vector is WHERE the attacker must be (network, adjacent, local, physical). Attack Complexity is HOW HARD the exploit is to execute

items.label: Skipping the rationale
items.text: A score without explanation is just a number. Document why you selected each finding and why you assigned each metric value

content.text: What’s the difference between CVSS Base, Temporal, and Environmental scores? Research how organizations use each type and when Environmental scores change the remediation picture.
Add: FIRST. CVSS v3.1 Specification Document. https://www.first.org/cvss/specification-document and NIST NVD CVSS Calculator. https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln-metrics/cvss/v3-calculator
A top-10 vulnerability table with CVSS 3.1 scores, severity ratings, and written rationale for your selection. This feeds directly into your Lab 5.2 Engagement Packet.

Chapter 20’s Qualitatively and Quantitatively Assessing Risk sections explain the underlying logic of scoring frameworks. CVSS is the industry implementation of the quantitative approach the text describes — translating vulnerability characteristics into a numeric score that enables objective comparison.

content.whatYoureDoing: Building a 5×5 risk matrix that plots your top 10 findings by likelihood and impact, then translating it into a prioritized remediation roadmap that accounts for LoneStar’s real-world constraints.
content.whyItMatters: Executives understand “red quadrant” better than “CVSS 9.8.” A risk matrix translates technical findings into business language. But a report that says “fix everything immediately” is useless — LoneStar has a 2-person DevOps team, no staging environment, and can’t take the portal offline during business hours. Your roadmap has to work in the real world.

content.title: CEO & Co-Founder, LoneStar DevOps
content.quote: I present to Longhorn Ventures in three weeks. I need to know what could actually hurt us and what the plan is to fix it. Give me something I can hand to investors.

steps.text: Define Likelihood Levels (1–5): Rare → Unlikely → Possible → Likely → Almost Certain

Define Impact Levels (1–5): Negligible → Minor → Moderate → Major → Catastrophic. Consider LoneStar’s context: $12M ARR, 200+ customers, Series B preparation, customer credentials and API keys in the database
Calculate Risk Score for each finding: Risk = Likelihood × Impact. Plot all 10 on a color-coded 5×5 grid: Critical (15–25), High (10–14), Medium (5–9), Low (1–4)

Build a prioritized remediation roadmap. Account for constraints: 2 DevOps engineers, no staging environment, zero-downtime requirement for the customer portal, 4-week timeline before enterprise review. Sequence by: risk score, remediation complexity, and quick wins that demonstrate progress to Amanda

content.title: Lead DevOps Engineer, LoneStar DevOps
content.quote: It’s just me and Miguel, and we can’t flip a switch and fix everything overnight. Give me quick wins I can show Amanda next week, and a realistic plan for the harder stuff.

content.description: Completed 5×5 risk matrix with findings plotted.
content.body: Take Screenshot 09. Capture your completed risk matrix showing all 10 findings plotted with color coding. A Word table, spreadsheet, or hand-drawn grid photographed clearly are all acceptable.

Take Screenshot 10. Capture your remediation roadmap showing sequenced findings with rationale. It must show at least: priority order, estimated remediation complexity (easy/medium/hard), and a brief justification for your top 3 priorities.

items.label: Ranking by CVSS alone
items.text: Business impact, asset criticality, and remediation complexity all affect real priority. MySQL default creds beat a high-CVSS vuln on a non-critical service

items.label: No quick wins
items.text: Include some easy fixes (disable the jdavis account, change default passwords) to give Sarah immediate action items

items.label: Unrealistic timelines
items.text: A 2-person team cannot remediate 10 critical findings in one sprint. Sequence realistically

content.text: How do you communicate risk to non-technical stakeholders? Research executive risk reporting and how to frame technical findings for board-level audiences.
Add: CIS Controls v8. Center for Internet Security. https://www.cisecurity.org/controls and ISACA Risk IT Framework. ISACA. https://www.isaca.org/resources/risk-it

A color-coded 5×5 risk matrix with all 10 findings plotted, and a sequenced remediation roadmap with rationale that accounts for LoneStar’s constraints. These are the core deliverables of your Lab 5.2 Engagement Packet.

Chapter 20’s Qualitatively Assessing Risk and Risk Management Best Practices sections explain the methodology behind what you just built. Pay particular attention to Probability/Threat Likelihood and Risks Associated with Cloud Computing and Virtualization — both map directly to your LoneStar findings.
Take the Lab 5.1 Quiz in Canvas. The quiz verifies you completed the hands-on work — questions will ask about specific outputs you should have seen. Keep your screenshots handy while taking the quiz.

items.label: Attack Surface Mapping
items.text: How to systematically discover every entry point — open ports, outdated services, misconfigured applications.

items.label: Vulnerability Chaining
items.text: Individual findings combine. MySQL default creds + jdavis sudo account = full environment access + former employee with competitive motive.

items.label: Exploitation Path Prioritization
items.text: Attackers don’t target the highest CVSS first — they target the most accessible path to the most valuable data.

items.label: Asset Inventory
items.text: You can’t protect what you don’t know exists. Accurate service mapping is the defensive foundation.

items.label: Risk Communication
items.text: Technical findings only drive action when stakeholders understand the business impact. The risk matrix is a translation tool.

items.label: Constraint-Aware Remediation
items.text: A perfect remediation plan that ignores team capacity and operational constraints will never be implemented.

purpleTeam.connection: This lab is the full assessment lifecycle in one pass: discover, score, prioritize, communicate. Red team finds the paths; blue team closes them. The deliverables you build here — the vulnerability inventory, CVSS table, risk matrix, and roadmap — serve both audiences simultaneously. Your Engagement Packet next week is about presenting that work to a client who needs to act on it.

finalChecklist.intro: Verify you have completed the following before taking the Lab 5.1 Quiz:

screenshotTable.description: docker ps showing all four containers running
Successful connectivity test to at least one service
Nmap host discovery with all container IPs identified
Combined -sV -sC scan output showing services, versions, and NSE findings
MySQL credential test or SSH jdavis account finding
CVSS 3.1 calculator with one finding scored
Top-10 vulnerability table with CVSS scores and selection rationale
Completed 5×5 risk matrix with findings plotted and color coded

items: All four Docker containers running and services accessible
Host discovery scan completed with all container IPs identified
Combined -sV -sC scan completed for all hosts
Top 10 findings selected with written rationale
All 10 findings scored using CVSS 3.1 calculator
5×5 risk matrix completed and color coded
All 10 screenshots saved in Module5_Screenshots folder

channels.label: Discord
channels.detail: Post in #lab-help for technical issues (fastest response)

needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late submissions are not accepted. The course drop policies exist for emergencies — use them wisely. This lab is eligible to be dropped, but don’t skip the vulnerability assessment skills it builds.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
prohibited: Generate fake screenshots or scan outputs
Complete the hands-on scanning or analysis work for you
Assign CVSS scores without you working through the calculator
genAI.principle: Think of AI like a tutor, not a ghostwriter. It can help you understand and improve, but the work itself must be yours.

whatsNext.title: Lab 5.2: Risk & Vulnerability Assessment Report
whatsNext.text: Next week you’ll compile everything from this lab into a professional Engagement Packet for LoneStar DevOps. Amanda needs a report for investors; Sarah needs a roadmap for her team. You already have the building blocks — Week 2 is about assembling and presenting them.
